title_en: The Difference Between the Shirk of Ancients and Modern Shirk
title_ar: الفرق بين شرك الأقدمين والشرك المعاصر
title_id: Perbedaan antara Syirik Orang-Orang Terdahulu dan Syirik Masa Kini
content_id:
Salah satu klarifikasi terpenting dalam seluruh subjek ini menyangkut perbandingan antara syirik kaum musyrikin Arab kuno dan syirik yang muncul di antara sebagian kaum Muslim di masa-masa selanjutnya. Banyak orang berasumsi bahwa karena para Sahabat Nabi memerangi kaum musyrikin Arab yang menyembah berhala, dan karena orang-orang yang mengunjungi kuil-kuil hari ini tidak menyembah patung-patung, kedua situasi tersebut sama sekali berbeda. Penalaran ini adalah kesalahan serius dan harus dibahas dengan hati-hati.

Alquran sendiri memberitahu kita tentang perilaku kaum musyrikin kuno dalam momen-momen krisis yang nyata: 'Dan apabila gelombang menutupi mereka seperti atap, mereka berdoa kepada Allah dengan ikhlas beragama kepada-Nya. Tetapi ketika Dia menyelamatkan mereka ke darat, sebagian dari mereka bertindak moderat. Dan tidak ada yang menolak tanda-tanda Kami kecuali setiap pengkhianat yang tidak bersyukur' (Alquran 31:32). Ini adalah pola yang berulang di seluruh Alquran — ketika kaum musyrikin menghadapi bahaya nyata di laut, ketika kematian terasa dekat, mereka meninggalkan berhala-berhala dan perantara-perantara mereka dan hanya menyeru kepada Allah. Dalam momen-momen rasa takut yang eksistensial, fitrah mereka — sifat manusiawi bawaan — mengalahkan kesyirikan mereka dan mereka mengarahkan doa mereka sepenuhnya kepada Allah.

Ini mengungkapkan sesuatu yang penting: kaum musyrikin kuno mempertahankan perbedaan antara waktu kemudahan dan waktu krisis. Dalam kemudahan, mereka menyeru berhala-berhala dan perantara-perantara mereka. Dalam krisis, mereka kembali kepada Allah saja. Syirik mereka terutama merupakan tindakan kemudahan dan tradisi agama daripada penggantian total iman kepada Allah.

Sekarang pertimbangkan situasi yang diamati pengarang pada zamannya sendiri dan yang berlanjut hari ini di beberapa komunitas Muslim. Orang-orang yang mengunjungi kuil-kuil dan menyeru mereka yang dikuburkan di sana tidak membatasi praktik ini pada waktu-waktu kemudahan. Mereka menyeru para wali dan orang-orang saleh yang telah meninggal dalam momen-momen kesusahan, penyakit, ketakutan, dan kebutuhan mendesak — tepat momen-momen di mana orang-orang Arab kuno kembali kepada Allah. Seorang wanita yang anaknya sakit menyeru seorang wali yang dimakamkan. Seorang pria yang menghadapi kehancuran finansial bernazar di sebuah kuil. Seorang musafir dalam bahaya berdoa kepada seorang wali daripada kepada Allah.

Dalam hal ini, bentuk syirik belakangan lebih berat daripada syirik kaum musyrikin kuno. Kaum musyrikin kuno, bahkan dalam kesyirikan mereka, memiliki cukup pengakuan akan kekuasaan eksklusif Allah untuk kembali kepada-Nya dalam krisis. Sebagian praktisi pengagungan orang-orang suci kontemporer telah kehilangan bahkan perbedaan ini. Mereka menyeru perantara-perantara bahkan ketika hanya Allah yang bisa membantu, yang merupakan kebingungan yang lebih dalam tentang sifat ketuhanan dan kekuasaan.

Ini bukan tuduhan terhadap semua kaum Muslim atau klaim bahwa setiap orang yang mengunjungi kuburan bersalah atas syirik. Mengunjungi kuburan untuk mengingat kematian dan untuk berdoa kepada Allah bagi almarhum adalah Sunnah yang terpuji. Nabi, shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam, mengunjungi kuburan Baqi dan mengajarkan para Sahabatnya sebuah doa untuk almarhum. Yang sedang dibahas di sini adalah menyeru almarhum untuk kebutuhan seseorang sendiri, meminta syafaat mereka, bernazar kepada mereka, dan mencari bantuan melalui mereka — tindakan-tindakan yang merupakan ibadah dan hanya milik Allah.

Memahami perbandingan ini membantu menghilangkan keraguan umum lainnya: bahwa karena orang-orang ini beriman kepada Allah dan mengucapkan La ilaha illa Allah, mereka tidak bisa bersalah atas syirik. Kaum musyrikin kuno juga beriman kepada Allah. Mereka juga mengakui kekuasaan dan penciptaan-Nya. Apa yang membedakan mereka sebagai kaum musyrikin bukan penyangkalan Allah tetapi pengarahan tindakan-tindakan ibadah kepada selain-Nya di samping-Nya. Syahadat yang dikombinasikan dengan tindakan-tindakan syirik tidak menyelesaikan kontradiksi — syirik tetap menjadi syirik dan harus ditangani melalui pendidikan, kejelasan, dan kembali kepada tauhid yang murni.
content_en:
One of the most important clarifications in this entire subject concerns the comparison between the shirk of the ancient Arab polytheists and the shirk that appears among some Muslims in later times. Many people assume that since the Companions of the Prophet fought the Arab polytheists who worshipped idols, and since the people who visit shrines today are not worshipping statues, the two situations are entirely different. This reasoning is a serious error and must be addressed carefully.

The Quran itself tells us about the behavior of the ancient polytheists in moments of genuine crisis: "And when waves come over them like canopies, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, some of them are moderate. And none rejects Our signs except every treacherous and ungrateful person" (Quran 31:32). This is a pattern repeated across the Quran — when the polytheists faced true danger at sea, when death felt near, they abandoned their idols and their intercessors and called upon Allah alone. In moments of existential fear, their fitrah — the innate human nature — overcame their polytheism and they directed their supplication entirely to Allah.

This reveals something important: the ancient mushrikeen maintained a distinction between times of ease and times of crisis. In ease, they called upon their idols and intercessors. In crisis, they returned to Allah alone. Their shirk was primarily an act of ease and religious tradition rather than a complete replacement of faith in Allah.

Now consider the situation the author observed in his own time and that persists today in some Muslim communities. People who visit shrines and call upon those buried there do not limit this practice to times of ease. They call upon the saints and the righteous deceased in moments of hardship, illness, fear, and desperate need — the very moments when the ancient Arabs returned to Allah. A woman whose child is ill calls upon a buried saint. A man facing financial ruin makes a vow at a shrine. A traveler in danger prays to a wali rather than to Allah.

In this respect, the later form of shirk is more severe than the shirk of the ancient Arabs. The ancient Arabs, even in their polytheism, had enough recognition of Allah's exclusive power to return to Him in crisis. Some contemporary practitioners of saint veneration have lost even this distinction. They call upon intermediaries even when only Allah could help, which is a deeper confusion about the nature of divinity and power.

This is not an indictment of all Muslims or a claim that everyone who visits graves is guilty of shirk. Visiting graves in order to remember death and to supplicate Allah for the deceased is a praiseworthy Sunnah. The Prophet, peace be upon him, visited the graves of Baqi and taught his Companions a supplication for the deceased. What is being addressed here is calling upon the deceased for one's own needs, asking them for intercession, making vows to them, and seeking relief through them — acts that constitute worship and belong to Allah alone.

Understanding this comparison helps dispel another common doubt: that because these people believe in Allah and say La ilaha illa Allah, they cannot be guilty of shirk. The ancient Arabs also believed in Allah. They also recognized His power and His creation. What distinguished them as mushrikeen was not a denial of Allah but the directing of acts of worship to others alongside Him. The testimony of faith combined with acts of shirk does not resolve the contradiction — the shirk remains shirk and must be addressed through education, clarity, and a return to pure tawhid.
content_ar: One of the most المهم clarifications in this entire subject concerns the comparison between the الشرك of the ancient Arab polytheists and the الشرك that appears among some المسلمون in later times. Many people assume that بما أن the الصحابة of النبي ﷺ fought the Arab polytheists who worshipped idols, and بما أن the people who visit shrines today are not worshipping statues, the two situations are entirely different. This reasoning is a serious error and must be addressed carefully. The القرآن الكريم itself tells us about the السلوك of the ancient polytheists in moments of genuine crisis: "And عندما waves come over them like canopies, they supplicate الله, sincere to Him in religion. But عندما He delivers them to the land, some of them are moderate. And none rejects Our signs except every treacherous and ungrateful person" (القرآن الكريم 31:32). This is a pattern repeated across the القرآن الكريم — عندما the polytheists faced true danger at sea, عندما الموت felt near, they abandoned their idols and their intercessors and called upon الله alone. In moments of existential الخوف, their fitrah — the innate human nature — overcame their polytheism and they directed their الدعاء entirely to الله. This reveals something المهم: the ancient mushrikeen maintained a distinction between times of اليسر and times of crisis. In اليسر, they called upon their idols and intercessors. In crisis, they returned to الله alone. Their الشرك was primarily an act of اليسر and religious التراث rather than a complete replacement of الإيمان in الله. Now consider the situation the author observed in his own time and that persists today in some مسلم communities. People who visit shrines and call upon those buried there do not limit this practice to times of اليسر. They call upon the saints and the الصالح deceased in moments of الشدة, المرض, الخوف, and desperate need — the very moments عندما the ancient Arabs returned to الله. A woman whose الولد is ill calls upon a buried saint. A man facing financial ruin makes a vow at a shrine. A traveler in danger prays to a wali rather than to الله. In this الاحترام, the later form of الشرك is more severe than the الشرك of the ancient Arabs. The ancient Arabs, even in their polytheism, had enough recognition of الله's exclusive power to return to Him in crisis. Some contemporary practitioners of saint veneration have lost even this distinction. They call upon intermediaries even عندما only الله could help, which is a deeper confusion about the nature of divinity and power. This is not an indictment of all المسلمون or a claim that everyone who visits graves is guilty of الشرك. Visiting graves من أجل remember الموت and to supplicate الله for the deceased is a praiseworthy السنة. النبي ﷺ, ﷺ, visited the graves of Baqi and taught his الصحابة a الدعاء for the deceased.
